_title: IEC 60364-4-43 Clause 434.5 — Fault Current Cable Protection (Adiabatic Equation)
_basis: IEC 60364-4-43:2008 Clause 434.5.2. k values derived from IEC 60364-5-54 Table 54.2 and IEC 60364-4-43 informative annex.
_note: This file covers the adiabatic check — verifying that the cable conductor survives the thermal energy of a fault current before the protective device clears it. The check must be applied at BOTH ends of the cable: for short-circuit faults at the remote end (minimum fault current, maximum device operating time) and at the origin end (maximum fault current, minimum operating time / let-through energy).

variables.t: Duration of fault current in seconds — the operating time of the protective device at fault current I
variables.S: Cross-sectional area of the conductor in mm²
variables.k: Material constant (see tables below) — depends on conductor material and insulation type
variables.I²t: Energy let-through of the protective device — obtained from manufacturer's data for currents above the instantaneous trip threshold

important_notes: For currents above the instantaneous trip threshold of an MCB, the device operates in the magnetic region (<0.01s) and the actual I²t let-through is far less than I² × 0.01. Use manufacturer's I²t let-through data for accurate checking.
For currents in the thermal region (long operating times), the formula applies directly: I²t = I² × t_trip from the time/current curve.
The adiabatic equation assumes all heat is retained in the conductor (adiabatic = no heat loss). This is conservative and valid for fault durations < 5 seconds.
For fault durations > 5 seconds, heat loss to insulation becomes significant — more detailed calculation is needed.

k_values_for_conductors._basis: k = √(Qc × ρ × (θf - θi)) where Qc = volumetric heat capacity of conductor, ρ = resistivity, θf = final temperature limit, θi = initial temperature

PVC_insulation_70C.note: Copper conductor with PVC insulation. k = 115 is used for cables with maximum operating temperature 70°C (PVC). Use for overload/fault protection check.

XLPE_EPR_insulation_90C.note: Copper conductor with XLPE or EPR insulation. k = 143 for cables with maximum operating temperature 90°C. Use for overload/fault protection check.

mineral_insulated_MICC_bare_70C_sheath.note: MICC cable with PVC sheath, rated 70°C sheath temperature.

mineral_insulated_MICC_bare_105C.note: MICC cable bare or with a high-temperature sheath.

CPC_bare_protective_conductor.note: For protective conductors (CPCs). If CPC is the same cable as the circuit (T&E or multicore), use 115 for PVC insulation. Bare CPCs exposed in cable trays or in conduit use k = 115. Bare buried conductors use k = 80 (lower because soil moisture limits temperature rise).

PVC_insulation_70C.note: Aluminium conductor with PVC insulation. Significantly lower k than copper — adiabatic check is more onerous for aluminium.

PVC_insulated.note: Steel conductors (cable armour used as CPC — not recommended but sometimes used). Very low k — check is onerous.

example_1_MCB_thermal_region._title: 4mm² copper PVC cable, 25A Type B MCB, fault at remote end — thermal region
example_1_MCB_thermal_region.scenario: Long circuit (30m). Short-circuit current at remote end = 100A (in thermal trip region of MCB). MCB operating time at 100A from time/current curve ≈ 40s.
example_1_MCB_thermal_region.action: Increase conductor cross-section. Required S ≥ √(400000) / 115 = 632.5 / 115 = 5.5mm². Select 6mm². OR reduce circuit length to lower the fault impedance and increase fault current (to push into instantaneous region).
example_1_MCB_thermal_region.lesson: Thermal region faults at low fault current are the dangerous case — the MCB takes a long time to clear but the fault energy is still significant. Design must ensure either: (a) fault current is high enough to push into instantaneous trip, or (b) conductor is large enough to absorb the energy.

example_2_MCB_instantaneous_region._title: 2.5mm² copper XLPE cable, 16A Type B MCB — instantaneous region fault at origin
example_2_MCB_instantaneous_region.scenario: Maximum fault current at DB origin = 3000A (well above 5 × 16A = 80A). MCB is in instantaneous trip region. Manufacturer's I²t let-through data: 6kA MCB lets through approximately 5000 A²s at 3kA symmetrical.
example_2_MCB_instantaneous_region.lesson: In the instantaneous region, MCBs are typically current-limiting. The I²t let-through is far less than I² × 0.01. The check almost always passes for cables of reasonable size when MCBs are current-limiting at the fault level. HRC fuses are even more current-limiting and give better cable protection at high fault levels.

example_3_CPC_sizing._title: Minimum CPC cross-section for a 4mm² copper XLPE circuit, 32A Type B MCB
example_3_CPC_sizing.scenario: Determine if a 1.5mm² CPC is adequate for a 4mm² circuit conductor.
example_3_CPC_sizing.worst_case_fault_current_at_MCB_instant: 32 × 5 = 160A (threshold for instantaneous trip). Use I²t from MCB time/current curve at 160A ≈ 0.01s
example_3_CPC_sizing._note: The rule-of-thumb is CPC ≥ S/2 for S ≤ 16mm² usually satisfies the adiabatic check without detailed calculation. For large conductors (≥ 25mm²), CPC may be reduced below S/2 but adiabatic check must confirm.
example_3_CPC_sizing.simplified_rule: For circuits where CPC is the same material and insulation as the line conductor: CPC = S for S ≤ 16mm². CPC = 16mm² for S = 25 or 35mm². CPC = S/2 for S ≥ 50mm².

minimum_CPC_cross_sections._title: Minimum protective conductor cross-sections (rule of thumb — adiabatic check still required for non-standard cases)
minimum_CPC_cross_sections._condition: CPC of same conductor material and insulation type as line conductor

prospective_short_circuit_current._title: Determining PSCC at points in the installation

at_service_origin.method: Measure using a loop impedance tester or a calibrated PSCC meter at the supply terminals.

typical_values.urban_high_density: Up to 16–25 kA at service entrance
typical_values.note: Always obtain PSCC from the supply authority or measure on site. Estimated values must be verified before final design.

at_downstream_point.Zs_upstream: Earth fault loop impedance at the origin of the protected cable (Ze + upstream cable impedance)
at_downstream_point.note: Calculate the PSCC at the origin of each protected cable to verify the protective device's breaking capacity is not exceeded.

minimum_fault_current_at_remote_end.note: This minimum fault current must be sufficient to trip the protective device within the required disconnection time from Table 41.1.
